questions.passage: The museum audio guide has long been accused of replacing looking with listening. A visitor stands before a painting while a polite voice supplies dates, patrons, quarrels, and symbolic meanings; the eye, supposedly freed from ignorance, may in fact become obedient. The complaint has force. A painting can turn into an illustration of a script, and the visitor may leave remembering the narrator's confidence more vividly than the color, scale, or strangeness of the work. Yet the accusation is too simple if it assumes that unassisted looking is pure. People bring scripts with them even when no headset is present: school memories, postcard images, museum brochures, and the silent fear of missing whatever cultured people are supposed to see. The real question is not whether mediation should exist, but whether it teaches attention or substitutes for it. A good audio guide does not tell the visitor what to admire; it gives the eye a task. It may ask the listener to compare the softness of a hand with the rigidity of a chair, or to notice how a background figure seems more alert than the saint in the foreground. Such guidance can slow perception. It turns information into an invitation rather than a verdict. Bad guidance has a different rhythm. It explains before the visitor has encountered any uncertainty. It names a symbol before the form has had time to trouble the viewer. It treats the artwork as a locked cabinet and the curator as the person carrying the key. The result is not knowledge but premature closure. The visitor has been relieved of embarrassment, but also of discovery. This distinction matters because museums are public institutions, not private exams in sophistication. A demand that visitors simply look can be ungenerous to those who have been taught that art is a code designed to expose their inadequacy. Interpretation can democratize attention by admitting that nobody arrives innocent. Still, democratization can become patronizing if the institution mistakes access for surrender. The visitor deserves help, not replacement perception. The best audio guide therefore resembles a good teacher who knows when to fall silent. It frames a problem, points to a detail, and then withdraws long enough for the visitor to test the claim against the surface before her. Its authority is real but provisional. It does not abolish the encounter between viewer and object; it makes that encounter less lonely and less scripted at the same time. The best audio guides therefore behave less like substitute teachers than like courteous companions. They slow the visitor at moments she might otherwise hurry past, but they do not exhaust the work by explaining it. A good guide may mention that a saint's gesture echoes an older devotional convention, then fall silent while the viewer notices how awkwardly the painted hand touches the frame. That silence is not a failure of information. It is the space in which looking resumes. The worst guides, by contrast, make attention unnecessary by rewarding the listener for having received the sanctioned point. In that case, the visitor leaves with more facts and less experience. The distinction matters because museums already encourage a form of anxious consumption: see the masterpiece, read the label, move on. An aid that deepens looking resists that anxiety; an aid that replaces looking perfects it.
questions.stem: The passage’s central claim is that museum audio guides are most valuable when they:

choices.A: replace uncertainty with a complete interpretation before the visitor looks.
choices.B: make difficult artworks easier by converting them into summaries.
choices.C: protect visitors from embarrassment by supplying the official meaning.
choices.D: direct attention without taking over the act of seeing.

explanations.A: The passage criticizes aids that settle meaning too early. Its preferred guide opens attention and then withdraws enough for the visitor to look.
explanations.B: The author does not value simplification for its own sake. A summary may be useful, but only if it returns the viewer to the work rather than making the work unnecessary.
explanations.C: Visitor anxiety is part of the museum setting, but the author is wary of aids that give people a sanctioned point before they have had an encounter with the object.
explanations.D: Correct. The passage distinguishes helpful guidance from substitution. A good guide slows the visitor, supplies context, and creates room for looking rather than replacing the encounter with a finished explanation.

questions.stem: The contrast between a guide as “courteous companion” and “substitute teacher” emphasizes the difference between:

choices.A: technology designed for children and technology designed for experts.
choices.B: assistance that enables attention and instruction that displaces it.
choices.C: spoken interpretation and printed labels as rival museum authorities.
choices.D: historical information and visual pleasure as incompatible aims.

explanations.A: The contrast is not about audience age or expertise. It concerns the role any guide plays in relation to attention.
explanations.B: Correct. The metaphor captures the passage’s main distinction. A companion helps the visitor remain present to the object, whereas a substitute teacher can become the primary experience and leave looking behind.
explanations.C: Printed labels are not the central opposition. The passage’s concern is broader: any explanatory aid may either support or replace the viewer’s encounter.
explanations.D: The passage does not oppose history to pleasure. It argues that historical information can deepen visual attention when used provisionally.

questions.stem: The example of the saint’s gesture primarily serves to:

choices.A: prove that religious paintings require expert knowledge to be appreciated.
choices.B: show that contextual information can lead back to visual noticing.
choices.C: illustrate why silence should be avoided in museum interpretation.
choices.D: argue that older conventions matter more than the viewer’s experience.

explanations.A: The passage does not claim expert knowledge is required for all appreciation. It values guidance when it assists looking without making the viewer dependent.
explanations.B: Correct. The example shows how a useful guide names a convention and then lets the viewer notice a particular painted detail. Information matters because it sharpens attention, not because it ends interpretation.
explanations.C: Silence is treated as necessary after the guide has done its limited work. The example praises a rhythm of explanation followed by renewed looking.
explanations.D: The older convention is useful only because it helps the viewer see the specific work. The passage does not subordinate experience to historical background.

questions.stem: The author would most likely criticize an audio tour that:

choices.A: points out a restored pigment and asks visitors to compare nearby surfaces.
choices.B: offers a brief historical note and then leaves several minutes without narration.
choices.C: tells visitors the correct emotional response to have before they view the work.
choices.D: admits that a painting’s meaning has been debated by later interpreters.

explanations.A: This would fit the author’s preferred model because it uses information to direct looking. It leaves the visitor with a task of attention.
explanations.B: This structure respects the author’s belief that silence can be productive. The guide assists and then withdraws.
explanations.C: Correct. This is the kind of substitution the passage resists. It gives the visitor a finished response before the encounter occurs, encouraging compliance instead of attention.
explanations.D: Acknowledging debate would likely deepen rather than flatten the encounter. The author objects to closure, not to interpretive complexity.

questions.stem: Which finding would most strengthen the passage’s argument?

choices.A: Many visitors say they prefer tours that tell them what each artwork means.
choices.B: Visitors remember more artists’ birth dates after listening to uninterrupted audio tours.
choices.C: Visitors given brief guides look longer than visitors given summaries.
choices.D: Museums with audio guides attract larger crowds than museums without them.

explanations.A: Preference for certainty might explain why substitute-style guides appeal to visitors, but it would not support the author’s normative claim about attention.
explanations.B: Remembering dates may show that audio conveys information, but the passage is concerned with whether information supports attention to the object.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage values guidance that returns viewers to the object. Longer looking after brief prompts would support the claim that limited explanation can deepen the encounter.
explanations.D: Attendance does not address the central distinction. A popular guide could still replace looking with consumption.

questions.stem: The author’s attitude toward audio guides is best described as:

choices.A: dismissive opposition to technological mediation in museums.
choices.B: uncritical enthusiasm for any tool that increases accessibility.
choices.C: nostalgic preference for museums without explanatory material.
choices.D: qualified approval shaped by concern for viewer independence.

explanations.A: The passage repeatedly grants that guides can be valuable. Its objection is to a particular use of mediation, not to technology as such.
explanations.B: Accessibility matters implicitly, but the author does not praise every aid. Some aids make experience thinner even while providing more information.
explanations.C: The passage is not nostalgic. It argues for a disciplined form of explanation that can exist within modern museum practice.
explanations.D: Correct. The author approves of audio guides when they assist the encounter and disapproves when they replace it. The stance is neither technophobic nor celebratory.

questions.stem: The passage would most likely agree that a museum aid fails when it:

choices.A: becomes the experience rather than a path back to the object.
choices.B: encourages viewers to notice details they had missed.
choices.C: admits that an object may support more than one interpretation.
choices.D: uses historical context to slow down a visitor’s movement.

explanations.A: Correct. The passage’s governing concern is substitution. A guide fails when listening to the guide replaces looking at the object.
explanations.B: That is a successful use by the passage’s standard. Noticing details is precisely what good guidance should enable.
explanations.C: Multiple interpretations are compatible with the passage’s preference for open attention. The author objects to premature closure.
explanations.D: Slowing the visitor can be valuable if it makes the encounter richer. The passage criticizes speed and consumption, not all mediation.
